coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent detection of tetracycline in foods based on carbon dots derived from natural red beet pigment
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Yuanyuan
Wang, Xinran
Bai, Huijie
Jia, Pei
Zhao, Yijian
Liu, Yingnan
Wang, Li
Zhuang, Yuting
Yue, Tianli
coredata.dc:description: As tetracycline (TET) residue does great harm to human health, it is urgently required to quantify it in animal-derived foods. In this paper, a fluorescent detection probe was synthesized by natural red beet pigment via one-pot hydrothermal method. The red beet pigment carbon dots (RBP-CDs) were evenly dispersed in water with an average diameter of 4.66 nm, and their surface were rich of amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. TET was able to quench the fluorescence of RBP-CDs within 5 min owing to inner filter effect (IFE) and photo-induced electron transfer (PET), and the quenching rate had good linear responses with the concentration of TET in the linear range of 0.5–30 μM and 30–90 μM, respectively. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.36 μM. Moreover, RBP-CDs had a good TET detection performance in food samples with complicated matrix, such as milk, egg and chicken. Due to its safe and easy synthesis procedure, good selectivity and high sensitivity, RBP-CDs will be put to wide use as a environment-friendly and rapid fluorescent probe in TET detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red beet pigment
Fluorescent detection
Tetracycline
Carbon dots

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid fundingbodyid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight oauserlicense openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder sponsoredaccesstype srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content oa subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid otherkwds primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of RBP-CDs (Inset: the corresponding particle size distribution histogram) and (b) HRTEM image.
(a) XPS survey spectrum for the RBP-CDs; (b) High-resolution spectra of C 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) N 1s.

(a) FTIR spectrum of RBP, RBP-CDs and RBP-CDs after addition of TET; (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of RBP-CDs (Inset: images of RBP-CQDs under daylight and UV light); (c) TGA and DTG curves of RBP-CDs.
(a) The fluorescence spectra of RBP-CDs reacting with different concentrations of TET; (b) The relationship between concentrations of TET and fluorescence quenching eﬃciencies.

Fluorescence quenching eﬃciencies of RBP-CDs after addition of TET and potential interferences (red bars); and fluorescence quenching eﬃciencies under their co-existence (blue bars). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of RBP-CDs and UV–vis spectra of TCs; (b) Observed and corrected quenching efficiencies of TET toward RBP-CDs; (c) Principle of the PET mechanism between TCs and RBP-CDs.
Illustration of Synthesis of RBP-CDs and Detection of TET.
The determination of TET in food samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent detection of tetracycline in foods based on carbon dots derived from natural red beet pigment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, 712100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, 712100, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As tetracycline (TET) residue does great harm to human health, it is urgently required to quantify it in animal-derived foods. In this paper, a fluorescent detection probe was synthesized by natural red beet pigment via one-pot hydrothermal method. The red beet pigment carbon dots (RBP-CDs) were evenly dispersed in water with an average diameter of 4.66 nm, and their surface were rich of amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. TET was able to quench the fluorescence of RBP-CDs within 5 min owing to inner filter effect (IFE) and photo-induced electron transfer (PET), and the quenching rate had good linear responses with the concentration of TET in the linear range of 0.5–30 μM and 30–90 μM, respectively. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.36 μM. Moreover, RBP-CDs had a good TET detection performance in food samples with complicated matrix, such as milk, egg and chicken. Due to its safe and easy synthesis procedure, good selectivity and high sensitivity, RBP-CDs will be put to wide use as a environment-friendly and rapid fluorescent probe in TET detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs), a series of antibiotics including tetracycline (TET), chlortetracycline (CTE), doxycycline (DOX), and oxytetracycline (OXY), have been widely used in animal husbandry for improvement of feeding efficiency and treatment of diseases due to its broad antibacterial activity, less toxicity and low cost (Huang et al., 2018; Wang, Jia, & Wang, 2017). Despite of obvious benefits, the long-term abuse of TET has induced significant hazards to animals and even human-beings such as bacterial resistance to antibiotics, allergic reactions, yellowing teeth and liver damage (Shao & Wu, 2020; Wang et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2020). Therefore, it is critical to monitor the residues of TET in animal-derived foods for insurance of food safety. Many countries and regions have restricted the residue of TET. For instance, the European Union (EU) and China have established the maximum residue limit of TET in milk as 100 ng/mL (Ge et al., 2018; Li et al., 2017) while the American Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has limited that to 300 ng/mL (Xu et al., 2018). Up to now, several analytical approaches such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Xu et al., 2017), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Moreno-González, Hamed, Gilbert-López, Gámiz-Gracia, & García-Campaña, 2017), mass spectrometry (MS) (László, Lányi, & Laczay, 2018), electrochemistry (Jalalian, Karimabadi, Ramezani, Abnous, & Taghdisi, 2018) and immunoassay (Bai et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2021) methods had been employed for quantified of TET. Despite these methods were precise and specific, the operational complexity, time consuming procedure, expensive cost and requirement of skilled personnel retrained their applications. Therefore, it is required to develop an efficient, economic and simple method for detection of TET in practical application.

Fluorescent analysis method displayed obvious advantages of easy operation and sensitive response, providing a better choice for TET monitoring (Liu et al., 2020). Recently, fluorescent nanomaterials such as quantum dots (QDs) (Han et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2020), carbon dots (CDs) (Long et al., 2019; Miao, Wang, & Yang, 2018), metallic nanoclusters (MNCs) (Li, Du, Zhang, & Huang, 2020; Zhang, Lv, et al., 2020), and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) (Li et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2018) have been widely explored. However, their further applications are limited by some inherent disadvantages. For example, heavy metal ions in QDs and MNCs are potential pollution for environment and the synthesis procedure of MOFs is complicated and expensive. Among these nanomaterials, CDs is attractive for excellent photostability, low toxicity and low cost (Kang & Lee, 2019; Lim, Shen, & Gao, 2015). They have been successfully applied in fluorescent detection of metal ions (Yue et al., 2019; Karami, Taher, & Shahlaei, 2020), pesticides (Li, Su, et al., 2020), small molecules (Shekarbeygi, Karami, Esmaeili, Moradi, & Shahlaei, 2021; Wang, Jana, Chung, & Hur, 2021) and so on.

Red beet pigment (RBP), named by firstly derived from red beets, is one of the widely used natural pigments in food industry because of its bright color and good water solubility (Amjadi, Ghorbani, Hamishehkar, & Roufegarinejad, 2018; Kosa & Zaheer, 2019). It is commonly used as colorant for jams, cold drinks and dairy products (Havlíková, Míková, & Kyzlink, 2010). Nowadays, researches about RBP are mostly focused on its extraction technology (Nestora et al., 2016) and nutrition functions (Esatbeyoglu et al., 2014), but there are few reports on its application in fluorescent detection. The pigment is easily obtained and cheap, but rich in carbon and nitrogen elements (Tutunchi, Roufegarinejad, Hamishehkar, & Alizadeh, 2019). Therefore, it has the potential to be used for synthesis of fluorescent CDs. The food grade precursor is environment-friendly and the CDs will be green and safe in detection and bioimaging.

Herein, red beet pigment carbon dots (RBP-CDs) were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method using RBP as carbon and nitrogen resource. Heating temperature, time, pH and concentration of pigment were optimized. The prepared CDs emitted blue fluorescence and showed selective response to TET. Upon the addition of TET, the fluorescence was quenched due to inner fitter effect (IFE) and photo-induced electron transfer (PET). The design was illustrated in Scheme 1 . Based on this, we developed a fluorescent method for detection of TET and this probe was applied for detection of TET in milk, egg and chicken samples with satisfactory accuracy and recoveries. The proposed method employing RBP-CDs as probe possessed some advantages: (1) The proposed probe had a comparable LOD and a relatively wide linear range under similar analysis time with reported fluorescence probe. (2) The probe was more environment-friendly and safe than that synthesized or modified with chemicals for using food grade pigment as precursor. (3) It was the first time for RBP-CDs used as a probe in detection of TET and had widen the application of natural pigments in detection. Therefore, RBP-CDs is a promising candidate for practical application in the future.

RBP was bought from Ketile Food Co. Ltd. (Kansas, USA). TET, CTE, OXY, DOX, azithromycin (AZM), ceftriaxone sodium (CFM), streptomycin sulfate (STR) and ﬂorfenicol (FFC) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Erythromycin (EM) and amoxicillin (AML) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HCl (37%) was purchased from Haohua Chemical Reagent Factory (Luoyang, China). NaOH, starch, ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), citric acid and Na2HPO4 were obtained from Guanghua Technology Co. Ltd. (Guangdong, China). NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, ZnCl2, MgCl2, AlCl3, and MnCl2 were bought from Kermel Reagent Co. Ltd. (Tianjin). Aspartic acid (Asp), lysine (Lys), cysteine (Cys), histidine (His), serine (Ser), tryptophan (Trp), phenylalanine (Phe), glucose and lactose were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). These chemicals were analytical regent grade and used as received without further puriﬁcation. Besides, all the solutions were prepared with ultrapure water during experiments.

RBP-CDs were synthesized through one-pot hydrothermal approach. RBP was chosen as the only precursor. Briefly, 2 g pigment powder was dissolved in 50 mL water with ultrasonic for 5 min. The solution was then transferred into a teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and then maintained at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the resulting brown solution was filtrated with 0.22 μm membrane filter. Finally, the obtained yellow solution was stored at 4 °C for future use.

The micrographs of RBP-CDs were characterized by Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded by Vetex 70 FT-IR spectroscopy (Bruker Corp., Germany). The thermal stability analysis was assessed on a thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) instrument (Netzsch STA 449F5, Germany) by heating from 30 to 800 °C. The heating rate was 10 °C/min with nitrogen as the atmosphere. UV–vis spectra were recorded using UV–vis 2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were performed on FluoroMax-4 spectrometer (Horiba, USA).

Fluorescent analysis was performed under the following procedures. In brief, 1 mL of the diluted RBP-CDs solution (40-fold) was mixed up with different concentrations of TET solution. And then the mixed solution was diluted to 3 mL with ultrapure water. At last, the pH of the system was adjusted to 7. After completely mixed under vortex for 30 s and incubation at 55 °C for 4 min 30 s, the emission spectra were recorded from 380 to 600 nm at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. All experiments were performed at least three times.

The food samples (milk, eggs and chicken) were purchased from local supermarket, and the pretreatment was carried out according to Chinese National Detection Standard (GB/T 21317–2007) with little modifications:

For milk sample, 5 g milk was diluted to 50 mL with 0.1 M EDTA-Mcllvaine buffer and put under vortex for 1 min at room temperature. Then the mixture was cooled to 4 °C after ultrasound for 20 min in ice water. After that, the mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm, 10 °C for 10 min. At last, the supernatant was filtrated by rapid filter paper and diluted 20 times with water before use.

For egg and chicken samples, 5 g homogenized sample and 20 mL EDTA-Mcllvaine buffer were added into a 50 mL centrifuge tube firstly. Then the mixture was under vortex for 1 min at room temperature followed by ultrasound for 20 min in ice water. And then the mixture was centrifuged at 3000 rpm, 10 °C for 5 min. After that, the precipitate was extracted for twice more as the above procedure with 20 mL and 10 mL EDTA-Mcllvaine buffer respectively. Last, the supernatant was collected together and diluted to 50 mL with EDTA-Mcllvaine buffer. The following treatment was the same as milk.

As the real samples were analyzed by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) to confirm that the nonexistence of TET in three samples (Fig. S6). A series amounts of TET were added into the prepared solution and then the detection measurements were carried out as described in section 2.4. EDTA-Mcllvaine buffer was prepared to chelate metal ions, proteins, amino acids and other substances in actual samples as well as extract the antibiotic components. The detection reliability of the probe was also verified by LC−MS. The differences between the methods were evaluated by t-test.

RBP is a kind of economic and environment-friendly natural pigment and it is also abundant of carbon and nitrogen element, which will be an ideal material for synthesis of carbon dots. To avoid tedious synthesis procedure, we presented a simple one-pot hydrothermal strategy. In order to obtain the best fluorescent performance, heating temperature, heating time, pH and concentration of pigment were optimized by evaluating the fluorescence intensity (FL intensity). Heating temperature and time were two essential factors in hydrothermal method for they may determine the degree of carbonization (Xia, Zhu, Feng, Yang, & Yang, 2017). Temperatures between 120 and 240 °C were investigated (Fig. S1a) and 200 °C was finally chosen for its highest FL intensity. Similarly, heating for 8 h was selected for the maximum of intensity among heating for 2–10 h (Fig. S1b). Lower temperature or less heating time caused incomplete carbonization of materials while over temperature or time led to excessive carbonization, under which the FL intensities were not satisfying. Besides, the pigment would be hydrolyzed at different degrees under different pH conditions (Tutunchi et al., 2019), which may lead to various structures and photoluminescence properties of as-prepared CDs. The pH of the pigment solution without adjustment was 3.6 and we further adjusted that to 5, 7, 9 and 11. As shown in Fig. S1c, the pH of 3.6 was chosen for the best FL performance. Moreover, the concentration of pigment was evaluated to synthesize the most CDs in per volume. It could be seen from Fig. S1d that the FL intensity reached to the maximum when the concentration was 40 mg/mL. The above results had determined the optimized synthesis parameters of RBP-CDs. And then, the concentration dependence on the FL intensity was performed. The as-prepared RBP-CDs was diluted for certain times with water, with the absorbance at 285 nm and FL intensity at 438 nm were measured. As shown in Fig. S2, the absorbance exceeded the maximum signal when the dilution ratio was less than 40. When the dilution ratio was more than 40, the absorbance decreased as the dilution ratio increasing. As for FL intensity, when the dilution ratio was less than 40, it increased as the increase of dilution ratio. It was probably because of the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect, which was a phenomenon that a significant amount of luminescence quenching occurs when a fluorophore is in a concentrated solution or in an aggregated state (Wu, Zhang, Yang, Bo, & Li, 2020). When the dilution ratio was more than 40, the FL intensity decreased with the increase of dilution ratio, which was determined by the amount of fluorophore in the solution. The FL intensity reached to maximum when the dilution ratio was 40, so the as-prepared RBP-CDs was diluted 40 times in further experiment.

The morphology and size distribution of as prepared RBP-CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 , the particles were well-dispersed and uniformly spherical nanoparticles (Fig. 1a) with an average particle size of 4.66 ± 0.5 nm. The higher solution TEM (HRTEM) image showed that RBP-CDs are amorphous without graphene structure (Fig. 1b). The elemental composition of RBP-CDs was analyzed by XPS spectra. The full range XPS spectrum showed three peaks located in 285 eV, 401 eV, and 533 eV which were attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively (Fig. 2 a). It indicated the successful introduction of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen element from RBP. The C 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum can be fitted into four peaks (Fig. 2b) located at 284.6 eV, 287.4 eV, 285.2 eV and 286.6 eV, corresponded to CC/C–C, CO, C–N, and C–O, respectively. Two peaks located in 531.4 eV and 533.2 eV in O 1s spectrum were attributed to O–CO while 532.4 eV was assigned to O–H (Fig. 2c). The N1s spectrum was composed of three peaks in 399.8 eV, 401.6 eV and 402.3 eV, ascribed to pyridine N, N–H and oxidized N respectively (Fig. 2d). The FT-IR spectra were recorded to analyze the surface chemical groups of RBP and RBP-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the characteristic peaks at 3425 cm−1 and 1605 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration and bending vibration of N–H in –NH2 group. And the peaks at 2966 cm−1 and 2925 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H in –COOH group, while the peaks at 2060 cm−1 and 2025 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of CC. Then the peak located at 1703 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of CO in –COOH. Two characteristic peaks at 1120 cm−1 and 1030 cm−1 corresponded to stretching vibration of C–O while the peak at 1385 cm−1 was bending vibration of O–H. The shift from 1030 cm−1–1120 cm−1 (C–O), and the appearance of new band at 1703 cm−1 (CO), 2025 cm−1 and 2060 cm−1 (CC) in RBP-CDs were attributed to carbonization of RBP in hydrothermal procedure. It also indicated that there were abundant groups like –OH, –NH2 and –COOH on the surface of RBP-CDs that imparted excellent FL and water-solubility, which were in accordance with the XPS results. These results revealed RBP-CDs have successfully synthesized by the simple one-step hydrothermal method. The method was superior in synthesis of CDs for its simplicity and safety with uniformly morphology and good water solubility products.

As for optical properties, UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 3b) had a typical absorption band centered at 285 nm, which corresponded to π−π* transition of CC (Chen et al., 2020). Besides, the excitation spectrum (purple area) had a peak at 350 nm which endowed the emission peak at 438 nm (blue area). The inset were the photos of RBP-CDs solution under daylight and UV lamp (365 nm) respectively. It could be observed that the solution was in bright yellow under daylight and exhibited blue fluorescence under excitation of 365 nm. Furthermore, the quantum yield was calculated to be 8.17% taking quinine sulfate as reference (Fig. S3), which was comparable with those carbon dots synthesized by hydrothermal method and employed carrots (7.6%) (D’Souza, Chettiar, Koduru, & Kailasa, 2018), apple juice (4.27%) (Mehta et al., 2015), bamboo leaves (7.1%) (Liu, Zhao, & Zhang, 2014) and lilac (6.5%) (Diao et al., 2018) as carbon source.

The thermal stability of RBP-CDs was evaluated by TGA. Fig. 3c showed the thermal decomposition pattern which could be divided into three stages by the peak points of derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) curve. At the early stage, there was 2.47% mass loss which was probably associated to the desorption of water molecules. In the second stage of 60–335 °C, the obvious mass loss might due to the decomposition of oxygenic functional groups in the surface of CDs. Further until the temperature raised to 800 °C, the 21.78% of loss was probably due to the disintegration of other functional groups and the carbon framework of CDs. The photoluminescent stability of RBP-CDs was also assessed (Fig. S4). The FL intensity remained above 80% even when the temperature increased to 65 °C (Fig. S4a). As shown in Fig. S4b, the FL intensity was almost unchanged within the NaCl concentration range of 0–1 M, indicating RBP-CDs could bear high salt concentration. And pH is also an important factor related to the fluorescence performance. Fig. S4c showed that the FL intensity remained almost constant over a range of 5–9. But in acid or alkaline condition, the ionization equilibrium (Dmitrieva, Ivanova, Semeikin, & Mamardashvili, 2020) in the solution may be broken, resulting the change of the surface group as well as FL intensity. Additionally, the decrease of FL intensity caused by the photobleaching is one of the important factors that trouble the photostability of quantum dots (Chen, Chen, Liu, & Li, 2015). The photostability was then evaluated by continuously irradiation at 350 nm. As presented in Fig. S4d, the RBP-CDs retained 91% of their original FL intensity even excited for 1 h, reflecting good excellent anti-photobleaching capability. These excellent features proved that RBP-CDs had great photoluminescent properties and it might be a promising probe in developing an effective fluorescent detection method.

In order to get the best detection performance, the excitation wavelength of RBP-CDs was optimized firstly. As shown in Fig. S5 when the excitation wavelength varied from 260 to 380 nm, the maximum emission position had a red shift, presenting an excitation-dependent FL behavior. The highest FL intensity was obtained at 350 nm. Hence, 350 nm was employed as excitation wavelength in following experiments.

Furthermore, some related parameters in FL detection including reaction pH, volume of RBP-CDs, reaction time and temperature were optimized (Fig. S7). Quenching eﬃciency (QE=(F0–F)/F0, where F0 and F were fluorescence intensities of RBP-CDs in the absence and presence of TET) was used to evaluate the detection performance of TET. The pH value is an important parameter in fluorescent detection because it may be related to the ionization equilibrium and affinity capacity of molecular (Liu et al., 2019) in solution, which influence the FL intensity a lot. Furthermore, it was reported that TET would undergo nucleophilic ring cleavage reaction under alkaline conditions to form degradation products with lactone structure (Zhao, Ci, & Chang, 1997). As shown in Fig. S7a, there was no obvious difference of QE under pH 3, 5, and 7. But in alkaline condition, the QE decreased which probably because of the disturbance of ionization equilibrium and changes in TET structure. It indicated that RBP-CDs had good potential in food sample detection for most food had a pH between 5 and 7. pH 7 was employed in following experiments. Furthermore, QE increased with the increasing volume of RBP-CDs for more CDs reacted with TET. And 1 mL was selected for little change was observed when the volume was more than 1 mL (Fig. S7b). The reaction time related to the degree of reaction. In order to mix the solution evenly, the beginning 30 s was under vortexed. As depicted in Fig. S7c, QE increased with reaction time and peaked at 5 min, therefore 5 min was chosen as the optimal time. Moreover, the influence of incubation temperature was assessed. The reaction may be more violent at higher temperature but high temperature may also reduce the FL intensity of CDs or destroy the structure of TET. QE achieved to the maximum at a temperature of 55 °C according to Fig. S7d. Therefore, the optimal detection parameters were selected as following: pH of the system was 7, the volume of RBP-CDs was 1 mL, reaction time was 5 min and temperature was 55 °C.

Under the optimized detection parameters, the sensitivity of the fluorescent probe was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the FL intensity of RBP-CDs at 438 nm decreased with the increase of TET concentration. Nevertheless, the QE increased as the concentration of TET increased (Fig. 4b). There were good linear relationships between the QE and concentration of TET in the range of 0.5–30 μM and 30–90 μM. The linear fits were y1 = 0.014x+0.020 (R2 = 0.997) and y2 = 0.007x+0.227 (R2 = 0.997), respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) (signal-to-noise ratio of 3) was calculated to be 0.36 μM (160 μg/L). The comparison of the analytical capabilities of RBP-CDs with electrochemical methods or other CDs was summarized in Table S1. Most probes were synthesized or modified by more than one kind of chemicals, while the synthesis of RBP-CDs was easy and safe for the precursor was only food grade pigment. Besides, under similar analysis time for no more than 5 min, RBP-CDs had a comparable LOD and relatively wide linear range compared with other probes. But all of these probes could not distinguish four kinds of TCs, which require further research in the future. All of this implied that the RBP-CDs was a desirable alternative for sensitive and efficient analysis of TET.

To investigate the selectivity of the proposed probe before applied in real samples detection, a variety of potential interfering substances coexisting in animal-derived foods including antibiotics (OXY, DOX, CTE, FFC, CFM, STR, AML, EM, AZM, AZI, ERY, FLO, CEF, AMO), metal ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+), amino acids (Cys, His, Ser, Lys, Asp, Try and Phe) and saccharides (glucose, lactose, and starch) were tested with a concentration of 100 μM. As shown in Fig. 5 , TCs induced significant fluorescence quenching, whereas other coexisting substances had slight inﬂuences on QE (except of iron ions) which could be ignored versus TET or even showed fluorescence enhancement (red bars). In addition, when the interfering substances (100 μM) coexisted with TET (100 μM), QE was affected a little (purple bars). Iron ions had effect on the detection performance, which was might because of their strong oxidizing property. They could easily react with oxygen-containing groups such as hydroxyl or carboxyl groups on the surface of RBP-CDs and obtain electron, causing the quenching of FL (Wu, Jiang, Gu, & Tong, 2017; Zhao, Zhao, Li, Ren, & Qu, 2014). It is noteworthy that in detection of TET, the interference of iron ions could be prevented for the samples contained iron were pre-treated by EDTA-McIlvaine buffer which had the ability to chelate metal ions. Thus, it indicated that RBP-CDs had excellent speciﬁcity for determination of TET in a complex system.

Based on the above results, we explored the possible mechanism of the reaction between RBP-CDs and TET. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the absorption spectra of TCs overlapped with the excitation spectra of RBP-CDs, indicating that IFE may be the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching (Jia et al., 2019). The IFE efficiency could be estimated according to the equation (Li et al., 2019):
CF =
F
c o r r
F
o b s d
=
2.3 d
A
e x
1 −
10
− d
A
e x
2.3 s
A
e m
10
g
A
e m
1 −
10
− s
A
e m
where Fobsd and Fcor represent the observed FL intensity of RBP-CDs at 438 nm and the corrected FL intensity respectively, and CF is the corrected factor. Aex and Aem are the absorbance of RBP-CDs with the addition of TET at 350 and 438 nm, respectively. g is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette (0.40 cm in this case), s is the thickness of the excitation beam (0.10 cm), and d is the width of the cuvette (1.00 cm). The CF of IFE at each concentration of TET were calculated in Table S2. The QE for the totally observed and the corrected of TET are given in Fig. 6b. It was found that approximately 56% quenching efficiencies were from the IFE, signifying another quenching mechanism coexisting.

Another mechanism of PET is hypothesized to induce FL quenching. The conduction band of RBP-CDs might be higher than the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of electron-acceptor TCs, leading to a driving force from RBP-CDs to TCs, resulting in FL quenching (Zhang et al., 2015; Arabi, Karami, Taher, & Ahmadi, 2020). In order to rationalize this, studies were carried out to calculate the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and LUMO energy levels of RBP-CDs. The band gap energy (Eg) and HOMO are calculated to be 3.54 eV and −5.23 eV by Kubelka–Munk plots and cyclic voltammetry (CV) studies, respectively (Fig. S8). Therefore, the LUMO and was estimated to be −1.69 eV. As evident in Fig. 6c, the favorable LUMO of TCs (Li, Yang, et al., 2020) facilitates the smooth PET from RBP-CDs to TCs, causing effective quenching.

The UV–vis spectra and FTIR analyses are employed to further explore the mechanism. As shown in Fig. S9, the blue line referred to RBP-CDs after treated of TET. There were two peaks at 271 nm and 359 nm owing to TET without any new peak or obvious shift. The FT-IR spectrum in the presence of TET (Fig. 3a) was in consistent with the UV–vis spectra, and there was no obvious change with that of RBP-CDs, indicating no new complex was formed. Therefore, the FL quenching of RBP-CDs after addition of TET was owing to IFE and PET.

To evaluate the applicability of the proposed method, three kinds of animal-derived foods including milk, chicken and eggs were spiked with TET to perform food samples detection. As demonstrated in Fig. S10, the LODs were 0.23 mg/L, 0.33 and 0.38 mg/kg in milk, egg, and chicken, respectively. The LOD changed slightly in food samples compared with that in standard TET solution, which might be attributable to complex matrix interference. In addition, some recoveries were presented in Table 1 , showing a range from 90.83% to 106.83% with relative standard deviations (RSD) no more than 6.22%. Besides, there was no significant difference between the proposed approach and LC−MS method by t-test (p > 0.05), which indicated the high stability and accuracy of the as-prepared sensor in food samples (Table S3). Moreover, compared with other reported fluorescent probes with recoveries between 85.15 and 113.47%, recoveries of the proposed probe were comparable and satisfactory (Table S4). It indicated that this probe would work as an available alternative with high accuracy and good repetition in practical application.

In summary, RBP-CDs with good water-solubility was synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method employing natural RBP as precursor. The nanoparticles were evenly sized sphere with plenty of functional groups on surface and possessed excellent photoluminescent properties and stability, including anti-photobleaching ability, salt tolerance, and pH stability. Based on this, a sensitive and selectively fluorescent detection method was developed for detection of TET owing to the IFE and PET between TCs and RBP-CDs. Under optimal conditions, the probe quantified TET in the range of 0.5–90 μM with a LOD of 0.36 μM. In addition, the feasibility of the probe was successfully verified by detecting TET in milk, chicken, and egg with satisfactory recoveries between 90.83% and 103.33%. RBP-CDs had great potential to be applied in practical detection of TET and this paper provided a new perspective for extending the application of natural pigments in the field of detection.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Yuanyuan Cao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xinran Wang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Huijie Bai: Formal analysis. Pei Jia: Validation, Visualization. Yijian Zhao: Validation, Visualization. Yingnan Liu: Validation, Visualization. Li Wang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Yuting Zhuang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Tianli Yue: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by Fundamental Research Funds for the Northwest A&F University (Z111021601) and Talented Program (A279021724).
